caveat: All figures are contested, lag events on the ground, and mix belligerent claims with independent estimates. Treat every number as an order of magnitude, not a fact.
background: The 2026 Iran war began on 28 February when the United States and Israel launched Operation Epic Fury — coordinated strikes that killed Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei in the opening hours. Iran retaliated against Israel, US bases, and Gulf states; Hezbollah opened a Lebanon front days later. A memorandum signed 17 June at Versailles and Tehran collapsed on 8 July, and fighting has since re-centered on control of the Strait of Hormuz, which the IRGC declared closed on 12 July.

status.headline: Trump eyes Iran's frozen assets and deploys a B-1 as strikes hit a 13th night; UK bases declared 'legitimate targets'; House votes 214-208 to end the war, Senate GOP lets it continue
status.detail: The war escalated on new fronts while its largest threatened blow still loomed. President Trump said the war's 'damages' would be 'paid for by Iranian money' the US 'has in its possession, and controls' — a threat to convert frozen Iranian assets into war reparations that Tehran's foreign ministry called 'incendiary,' with foreign minister Abbas Araghchi warning 'mindless aggression' would not help produce a deal. Trump said he remained close to deciding whether to launch 'a massive attack' larger than the campaign so far — widely read as the deeply buried 'Pickaxe Mountain' (Kuh-e Kolang) nuclear complex near Natanz — as Axios reported the US deployed a B-1 bomber toward the Middle East 'as attacks on Iran intensify,' lending the threat physical shape. CENTCOM said it completed a thirteenth consecutive night of strikes; Iran's Revolutionary Guard said it stopped three oil tankers from passing through the Strait of Hormuz, and branded UK bases used by American bombers 'legitimate targets' — claiming a base in south-west England had launched strikes — prompting Britain to say it is 'ready to defend itself.' In Washington, the House of Representatives passed a war-powers resolution directing Trump to end the conflict by 214-208, with four Republicans crossing — its second such rebuke — but Senate Republicans voted to let Trump keep waging the war, so no binding brake reached his desk, and Senator Rand Paul said the US is 'worse off than we were before'; the votes followed the House adopting a roughly $95bn war budget and a $1.15tn military bill, the House's answer to Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth's request for nearly $88bn more (about $67bn to restock munitions) atop a war he put at $37.5bn. Brent crude held above $100 a barrel as the Houthis pressed a second front against Saudi tankers in the Red Sea and war-risk insurance for Hormuz transits ran roughly four times its five-year average. The confirmed US toll stands at a contested 17–18 dead and nearly 100 wounded, with the New York Times reporting a Pentagon website appears to show a lower figure than tallies compiled elsewhere.

stats.label: Killed in Iran (Iranian figures)
stats.note: US/Israeli estimates put Iranian military deaths alone above 6,000

stats.label: US service members killed
stats.note: An ABC News/Time tally put the toll at 17; on 23 July the Guardian named four of the fallen among '18 US troops killed' — the count creeping up in a war fought across several countries. On 24 July the New York Times reported the toll shown on a Pentagon website appears lower than tallies compiled elsewhere. Includes those killed at Jordan's Muwaffaq Salti Airbase — where the WSJ reports the Iranian missile struck US personnel housing — and Sgt Michael Swinton of Fayetteville, NC, killed in Iraq during a 'controlled detonation' of a downed Iranian drone

stats.label: US service members wounded
stats.note: The Pentagon admitted nearly 100 US troops have been wounded over two weeks of escalating strikes, 21 July — a day after the New York Times reported dozens of injuries had been withheld

stats.label: Left without water in southern Iran
stats.note: Iranian figure after US strikes on water infrastructure, 18 July

stats.label: Iranian naval vessels destroyed or damaged
Direct US war cost so far
stats.note: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth's figure to the Senate on 21 July; he requested roughly $88bn more (about $67bn to restock depleted munitions). On 23 July the House passed a ~$95bn budget to fund the war and a $1.15tn military bill, largely along party lines, both facing an uncertain Senate. On 24 July Trump said the war's 'damages' would be 'paid for by Iranian money' the US controls — a threat to tap frozen Iranian assets that Tehran called 'incendiary.' A separate running estimate put the broader cost to US taxpayers above $113bn as of June 2026

stats.label: Global oil supply disruption
stats.note: Hormuz carries roughly a fifth of global oil flows

stats.label: Crude oil price
stats.note: Brent broke above $100 a barrel on 23 July — its highest of the war and the first time in two months — and held there into 24 July, after Houthi attacks on Saudi tankers, the Bab al-Mandeb 'Gate of Tears' threat, Iran's claim to have stopped three tankers in Hormuz, and Trump's talk of a 'massive attack' on Iran; per the Guardian and CNBC

stats.label: Hormuz war-risk insurance
stats.note: War-risk insurance for tankers transiting the Strait of Hormuz is running roughly four times its five-year average, per Al Jazeera (23 July) — a cost that flows into freight and, in time, pump prices

stats.label: US gasoline price
stats.note: US average gasoline topped $4 a gallon on 20 July (per ABC News); diesel has passed $5 — the war's cost reaching American drivers

stats.label: Strait of Hormuz status
stats.note: Declared closed by the IRGC on 12 July; now a live shooting zone — the US striking nightly (a 13th consecutive night by 24 July) to force it open, Iran attacking and halting tankers to keep it shut. On 24 July the IRGC said it stopped three oil tankers from passing through the strait. On 22 July Trump vowed to destroy an Iranian bridge or power plant for each Iranian attack in the strait; Iran warned it would answer 'eye for an eye' and by blacking out US-host Gulf states

stats.label: Houthi Red Sea blockade
stats.note: Declared against Saudi Arabia 20 July and extended to Bab el-Mandeb by 22 July; on 23 July the Houthis attacked a Saudi oil tanker in the Red Sea and claimed a second — turning the blockade kinetic — and threatened to make Bab el-Mandeb, the 'Gate of Tears,' a second live chokepoint. Trump vowed to 'take care of' the group — a second maritime front alongside Hormuz

stats.label: IAEA safeguards access
stats.note: Iran declared normal safeguards 'legally untenable' after strikes on Natanz; on 18 July it also suspended its commitments to the interim deal with the US

stats.label: 'Pickaxe Mountain' (Kuh-e Kolang)
stats.note: On 22 July Trump threatened to hit this underground complex near Natanz 'very heavily'; it is estimated to be buried almost 100 metres inside a mountain, and little is publicly known about it. Widely read as the likely target of the 'massive attack' Trump says he is close to ordering; on 24 July Axios reported a B-1 bomber — able to carry the largest conventional bunker-busters — deployed toward the region as strikes intensified

stats.label: Bushehr nuclear power plant
stats.note: Satellite images show fresh damage from strikes dated 7–12 July; Bushehr is Iran's largest nuclear plant

actors.members: United States, Israel; Saudi Arabia, UAE, Kuwait and Bahrain after Iranian strikes; support from UK, Jordan, Qatar, Turkey and others
Iran (Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei, President Pezeshkian), Hezbollah, Houthis, Popular Mobilization Forces, Hamas, Palestinian Islamic Jihad
